findings.reachability: Any caller that passes an untrusted string to importKey() without specifying a format triggers autoImport() which runs all six regexes against the input. Reachable from any public NodeRSA constructor or importKey() call with a string argument.
findings.justification: All six PEM/OpenSSH autodetection regexes use the pattern `(?=(([A-Za-z0-9+/=]+\s*)+))` — a nested quantifier inside a lookahead where both the inner `[A-Za-z0-9+/=]+` and the outer `(\s*)+` can match whitespace, causing catastrophic backtracking on a crafted string of base64 characters not followed by the closing PEM marker. The /g flag with a stateful lastIndex compounds the issue on repeated calls.

detection_pattern.section_name: ReDoS: nested quantifier in PEM lookahead
detection_pattern.description: Matches the specific catastrophic backtracking pattern used in PEM format autodetection: a possessive-less nested quantifier inside a lookahead applied to base64-like input. Common in hand-rolled PEM parsers.

findings.reachability: All public encrypt/decrypt/encryptPrivate/decryptPublic calls pass through these two methods. Any caller with access to the NodeRSA instance can trigger these paths.
findings.justification: Both methods wrap their entire body in a bare `catch { throw new Error('Error during encryption/decryption') }`, erasing all internal exception detail. For decryption this is particularly dangerous: padding validation errors (from PKCS#1 v1.5 or OAEP) are silently collapsed into a single indistinct error, which (a) hides legitimate diagnostic information and (b) means callers cannot distinguish a padding failure from a key mismatch, facilitating a Bleichenbacher-style binary oracle if response timing differs between the swallowed exception paths.

detection_pattern.section_name: Error swallowing: generic catch in crypto operations
detection_pattern.description: Flags bare catch blocks in encrypt/decrypt functions that rethrow a generic error, hiding the original exception. This pattern can mask padding oracle timing distinctions and prevents callers from implementing proper error handling.

findings.reachability: Triggered when a caller invokes decrypt(ciphertext, 'json'). The decrypted bytes are attacker-controlled if the attacker controls the ciphertext or the key used. Reachable from any call to decrypt() or decryptPublic() with encoding='json'.
findings.justification: At line 3883, `JSON.parse(toUtf8(bytes))` is called on the raw decrypted bytes without any sanitization. If the decrypted payload contains `{"__proto__":{"isAdmin":true}}` or similar, `JSON.parse` in V8 will set properties on `Object.prototype`, polluting the prototype chain for all objects in the process. This is a standard JSON.parse prototype pollution vector that the library exposes as a first-class API feature.

detection_pattern.section_name: Deserialization: JSON.parse on untrusted decrypted bytes
detection_pattern.description: Flags raw JSON.parse calls in decryption output paths. Decrypted bytes may be attacker-controlled and can contain __proto__ or constructor keys that pollute the prototype chain in the calling process.

findings.reachability: Any caller who supplies a user-controlled string as the format argument to importKey() or exportKey(). In practice requires attacker control of the format parameter.
findings.justification: The lookup `EXPORT_FORMAT_ALIASES[format]` at lines 3795 and 3805 uses a user-supplied string as a plain-object property key without a `hasOwnProperty` guard or `Object.create(null)` map. Passing `'__proto__'` or `'constructor'` resolves to inherited Object prototype members rather than alias strings. The `?? format` fallback then passes the raw inherited value to `formatParse()` which calls `.split('-')` on it, throwing a TypeError on non-string values. While not exploitable for privilege escalation, it can cause unexpected crashes and is a prototype-access antipattern.

detection_pattern.section_name: Prototype lookup: user-controlled key on plain object
detection_pattern.description: Flags user-supplied strings used directly as property keys on plain object lookup tables without hasOwnProperty guards or null-prototype maps. Can leak prototype properties or cause unexpected behavior with __proto__/constructor keys.
